translation.content: What makes something sound like Dave Huismans? His music carries a signature you can't quite name, a tension that has defined every chapter of his career. And unlike most producers, he really does operate in phases. A man of many monikers, Huismans resurfaced this year after nearly a decade away with two new aliases, In Transit and ex_libris, which we named one of our favourite records of 2025. His latest projects, though sonically a world away from the work under his most recognised, 2562 and A Made Up Sound, share the same slipperiness, intricacy and disregard for tidy genre borders. Ask many of his contemporaries, and they'll tell you those records defined that era. It's perhaps captured best in Ben UFO's awestruck admission: "I've no idea how Dave does it." "Naiveté and a lack of prejudice… a really wide-eyed type of open-mindedness," Huismans once told RBMA, recalling his youthful forays into dance music, and that ethos is alive and well on his second RA Mix. Across the two hours, there's no shortage of atmosphere (Losoul's "Sunbeams and the Rain" is a particular highlight), but the through line is groove: soulful, vocal deep house into driving, techy depths; walls of glitchy drums and frantic percussion into guitar funk. It's a thrilling listen, traversing a map of influences from Madlib and gyrofield to Prince and Autechre. "I got carried away a bit," Huismans says about RA.1015 in the Q&A below. That's no bad thing. It sounds like someone enjoying himself again, and perhaps, after letting a few old skins fall away, Huismans is ready to shapeshift once more. And if it's anything like the last time, a lot of brilliant music awaits. - Bella Aquilina What have you been up to recently? It's a bit of a blur to be honest, I don't know where to start… it's been an intense time on so many levels. Besides anything music related, I've greatly enjoyed being part of the education team of Utrecht's city library and hanging out with my kids whenever I can. Can you tell us the idea behind the mix? I wanted to try and align it with my own recent musical output a little bit, while also giving an impression of how I like to DJ at the moment, including some contemporary favourites, some history / where I come from, a few all-timers that still never fail to move me—which obviously is far too many dots to join… I got carried away a bit. Somehow it still ended up as a pretty representative cross-section. How and where was the mix recorded? At home, using a Live setup as a three-channel DJ mixer with four-band EQs and mute switches. I'm quite a dinosaur with technology and used to be adamant about mixing the old-school way; I still do at shows. But after almost 30 years of having done that, I figured I'd allow myself to embrace the different possibilities Ableton Live opens up for recorded mixes. Never too old to learn. What's one social or political cause you want the world to pay more attention to? Everyone and everything suffering under the unprecedented right-wing destruction, war, fear-mongering and greed right now. In particular, the people of Palestine and Ukraine, who by all accounts are facing even more unfathomably grim times, without any justice in sight. It breaks my heart. As does the equally bewildering lack of shared urgency for the climate crisis, both in politics and society. You returned this year with two new aliases, ex_libris and In Transit—both a clear shift from your previous aliases. What sparked that change? I can see how it looks like a shift—it is. Yet internally it doesn't feel like that as much. It's been part of me all along; in some cases even made simultaneously. Maybe I just didn't feel room to present it before, or thought no one would be interested. It's quite possible these thresholds existed mainly in my own head. At this point, at my old age, I just wanted to have no regrets about things I didn't do. You've also come back to DJing. How does it feel being in the club again, and what’s different now? It's been very enjoyable, I've missed it. It's been liberating that it's not my livelihood anymore. I take every show as a gift and have felt welcomed to play how I like to. Bless the venues and promoters who let me and provide the right context for it. What's one club or party that had an impact on you as an artist? Let's go with a recent experience: Phono Arhus, where I got to play alongside Fergus Jones of FELT last month. Intimate cosy venue, wooden floor, great sound and most importantly a passionate, knowledgeable, lovely local community. I'd wish for every city to have such a safe, good vibes space. Fergus set the mood beautifully with his live performance and I could take it slowly from there. That was inspiring. Actually parts of this mix were informed by it. Your ex_libris 12" series has become one of the standout releases of the year—earthy, fluid and very intentionally crafted, right down to the limited runs and visual design. What was the core idea behind ex_libris, and what made this the right moment to present music in such a focused, handcrafted way? Thank you so much! I struggle to catch it in a few words; between the music, the sound sources, the titles and the artwork it kind of took up a life of its own. I am over the moon it's resonated so much, it is honestly the last thing I expected. About a year ago, all I had was a hard disk with music gathering dust and an inexplicable hunch that it was now or never. And it wouldn't have come together the way it did without my friends Thijs giving me the necessary kick up the arse and Bernie going above and beyond on the visual side. I cannot thank them enough. What are you looking forward to in the near future? A good, long winter sleep. I promise this time I'll be away six weeks rather than six years.
translation.blurb: A statement two hours from the Dutch artist also known as A Made Up Sound, 2562 and ex_libris.
